title: AI knows when it may be wrong and can recover
description: An AI system can estimate uncertainty, abstain when needed, detect its own errors, and improve after feedback instead of bluffing through risk.

subQuestions.title: Can it produce calibrated confidence?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether expressed confidence tracks actual correctness rather than sounding persuasive by default.
subQuestions.rationale: Calibration is improving, but many systems still sound too certain in the very cases where error rates are highest.

proofItems.title: Benchmark suite: Confidence calibration against answer correctness
proofItems.shortExplanation: Calibration benchmarks show meaningful progress, but still reveal overconfidence on harder and more ambiguous tasks.

subQuestions.title: Can it abstain when evidence is insufficient?
subQuestions.description: Tests whether the system declines or narrows an answer when the available evidence does not justify a confident response.
subQuestions.rationale: Abstention behavior exists, but it is still not reliably triggered in all of the cases where it should be.

proofItems.title: Controlled study: Abstention under insufficient evidence
proofItems.shortExplanation: Controlled evaluations show more frequent and more useful abstention than earlier systems, but still leave critical misses.

subQuestions.title: Can it detect internal contradictions?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether the system notices when its own intermediate claims or outputs conflict with one another.
subQuestions.rationale: Self-diagnosis works in many simpler cases, but hidden contradictions inside longer outputs still slip through too often.

proofItems.title: Expert blind review: Internal contradiction detection
proofItems.source: Expert Review Panel on AI Performance
proofItems.shortExplanation: Review panels found reliable detection of obvious inconsistencies, but weaker performance once contradictions depended on longer output context.

subQuestions.title: Can it self-correct after feedback or tool results?
subQuestions.description: Tests whether new evidence, critique, or tool output leads to an improved answer rather than defensive persistence.
subQuestions.rationale: Explicit critique and tool feedback often lead to materially better second-pass answers in a wide range of workflows.

proofItems.title: Controlled study: Second-pass improvement after critique
proofItems.shortExplanation: Repeated-answer studies show strong gains after critique or tool feedback, especially on factual and coding tasks.

subQuestions.title: Can it escalate uncertainty instead of bluffing in high-risk cases?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether the system hands off or narrows scope when risk is high and confidence is weak.
subQuestions.rationale: Escalation behavior is present in some high-stakes copilots, but trigger quality and consistency remain uneven.

proofItems.title: Deployment audit: High-risk uncertainty escalation
proofItems.shortExplanation: Operational audits show improving escalation behavior in bounded workflows, but also document cases where the model still pressed ahead too confidently.
